title: Which coalition won the 2026 Colombia Senate election?
rules: If the winner of the next Colombian Senate election scheduled to be held in 2026 (at the latest) is Government Alliance, then the market resolves to Yes.
tavily_brief: [redacted] Let me search for pre-cutoff polling and analysis:Perfect. Now I have good pre-cutoff data. [redacted] Pre-election polling from early February 2026 shows the Historic Pact commanding 24.3% of voting intention according to AtlasIntel's survey of 7,298 respondents (conducted January 27–February 4), with the opposition Democratic Center trailing at 20.5%. A separate Guarumo/Ecoanalítica poll from mid-January measured the Historic Pact at 27.1% versus the Democratic Center's 20.3%, confirming consistent polling leads of 4–7 percentage points. Seat projections based on these polls suggest the Historic Pact will secure 20–25 Senate seats, making it the largest single bloc in the 103-seat chamber, while the Democratic Center is projected to win 17–19 seats. Neither coalition will achieve an outright majority, but the Historic Pact's lead translates into a commanding position for coalition-building. The 2022 baseline shows the Historic Pact won 20 seats with 14.1% of the vote; the 2026 polling indicates substantially higher support (24–27%), suggesting seat gains are likely. President Gustavo Petro's approval rating reached 49.1% in late February 2026 (Invamer survey), his highest since taking office, up 11 points from November 2025. This momentum typically benefits the governing coalition in legislative elections. The Historic Pact also benefits from running a closed list strategy, which concentrates votes and reduces fragmentation compared to open-list competitors. The opposition Democratic Center, while organized, remains tethered to former president Álvaro Uribe's legacy and faces internal divisions over strategy. Traditional parties (Liberal, Conservative) have declined significantly and are fragmenting, with the Liberal Party polling at 11.6% and the Conservative at 8.5%—well below their 2022 levels. This fragmentation works in the Historic Pact's favor by preventing opposition consolidation. The election occurs before the May 31 presidential vote, and a strong legislative showing by the Historic Pact would provide momentum for its presidential candidate, Iván Cepeda, who leads presidential polls at 31–33%. **Most important facts:** (1) Historic Pact leads all pre-election polls by 4–7 points and is projected to win the most Senate seats, establishing it as the dominant legislative force; (2) President Petro's approval reached 49% in late February, providing tailwinds for the government coalition; (3) Opposition fragmentation and the Historic Pact's closed-list strategy reduce the likelihood of a surprise opposition victory.

The poll indicated Petro's party would not achieve Senate majorities despite leading.** --- **Note:** The remaining search results are all dated March 8-9, 2026 or later, reporting on actual election results rather than pre-election surveys, so they fall outside your specified date range and have been excluded per your instructions.

args.reasoning:
EVIDENCE INVENTORY:
- AtlasIntel poll (late Feb 2026): Pacto Histórico (Petro's government alliance) leads Senate voting intention at 28.2%, ahead of opposition forces.
- Guarumo/Ecoanalítica poll (Feb 2026): Pacto Histórico 27.1%, Centro Democrático (opposition) 20.3%, voto en blanco 11.3%. Note: poll indicates Pacto would lead but NOT achieve a majority.
- The Colombian Congress is highly fragmented, with multiple parties: Pacto Histórico (government), Centro Democrático (opposition), plus Liberal, Conservative, U Party, Cambio Radical, Verdes, etc., which are not formally either "government" or "opposition" but rather swing/independent blocs.

INTERPRETATION:
The question asks which coalition "won" the Senate election. If interpreted as plurality/largest force, the polling evidence converges that Pacto Histórico leads — so the Government Alliance is most likely the largest single bloc. However, the opposition (Centro Democrático plus aligned parties like Cambio Radical, and portions of traditional parties that have moved into opposition to Petro) could collectively exceed the government alliance if grouped together. Petro's approval has been declining, and traditional parties (Liberal, Conservative, U) have largely distanced themselves from the government.

UPSET CHECK: Petro's coalition has been fracturing throughout 2024-2025 with multiple cabinet exits and party defections. The Senate has been hostile to Petro's reforms. This suggests opposition could collectively outperform. However, as a single named coalition, Pacto Histórico leads polls.

The Kalshi market prices Government Alliance at 0.69, Opposition at 0.22. Given that polls show Pacto Histórico leading as the largest single force (consistent with "Government Alliance winning"), and that the resolution likely follows largest plurality, the market pricing seems reasonable. I'll lean slightly toward the market consensus but allow for the real possibility that opposition (broadly defined) wins, especially given Petro's declining popularity and the fractured government coalition.

Final: Government Alliance ~0.62, Opposition ~0.30 (with ~0.08 residual for other/unclear).

args.reasoning:
Evidence inventory:
- Government Alliance (Pacto Histórico and allies): ColombiaOne (Mar 2, 2026) reports that multiple pre‑election polls show a close contest for the Senate, with the Historic Pact and the Democratic Center as the leading forces six days before the vote. This places the governing Pacto Histórico at or near the top of Senate vote intention heading into election day, indicating it remains capable of winning the largest bloc. IFES Election Guide confirms the 2026 Senate election timing and participating parties, framing the contest in which Pacto Histórico is the principal government-aligned list.
- Opposition (Centro Democrático, Conservatives, Cambio Radical, etc.): Pulzo (Feb 26, 2026) cites an Invamer poll showing Centro Democrático senator Paloma Valencia leading the right‑of‑center “Gran Consulta por Colombia” with 41.6% against rivals like Carlos Fernando Galán and Juan Daniel Oviedo. While this is a presidential coalition primary snapshot, it signals energized right‑of‑center momentum and brand strength for Centro Democrático just before legislative voting, which could lift opposition Senate performance. ColombiaOne’s summary of polls likewise highlights the Democratic Center’s competitiveness in the Senate race.

Implications:
- The ColombiaOne polling synthesis indicates the governing Historic Pact is still among the two clear frontrunners for the Senate plurality; therefore, it retains a plausible edge to finish first in seats/vote share.
- The Invamer/Paloma Valencia surge is a concrete, named sign of opposition enthusiasm; therefore, the race is tighter than a comfortable government lock, and an opposition plurality is a live possibility.

Upset check: I do not see a singular shock (e.g., scandal, list disqualification) that would flip the race decisively; signals point to a competitive but not lopsided field.

Forecast: Lean Government Alliance to win the Senate plurality, but with meaningful opposition upset risk.

args.reasoning:
EVIDENCE INVENTORY:

*   **Government Alliance (Pacto Histórico):**
    *   **WEAK/MIXED:** President Gustavo Petro's personal approval rating has shown signs of recovery in mid-to-late 2024, with one poll showing a rise to 47% in July following the passage of a pension reform. (Source: ColombiaOne, Pulzo).
    *   **STRONG:** The governing Pacto Histórico coalition is experiencing significant internal divisions. In August 2024, multiple member parties, including MAIS, ADA, and Fuerza de la Paz, publicly resisted a proposal to merge into a single political party for the 2026 elections. This signals deep fissures and a lack of unity, which could weaken their electoral performance. (Source: Infobae, Semana).

*   **Opposition:**
    *   **MODERATE:** Opposition leaders, including influential former presidents César Gaviria and Álvaro Uribe, are actively working to build a broad, unified coalition for the 2026 elections. This suggests a coordinated and strategically serious challenge is being mounted. (Source: ColombiaOne).

IMPLICATION:
The evidence points toward a more competitive election than market prices suggest. While President Petro has seen a modest rebound in popularity, the structural integrity of his coalition is in question. The public and acrimonious disputes over forming a single party are a significant headwind, threatening to splinter their electoral efforts. Conversely, the opposition is showing signs of consolidation and strategic planning by bringing together major political figures. The organizational disarray within the Pacto Histórico is a more potent and durable signal than a temporary rise in presidential approval. This internal conflict provides a clear opening for a unified opposition to gain ground.

UPSET CHECK:
The deep, public infighting within the governing coalition is a strong signal that justifies a significant departure from the market favorite. This is not a minor disagreement but a fundamental conflict over the coalition's future, which could lead to a weaker, more fragmented campaign. This creates a substantial probability for an opposition victory.

SYNTHESIZED SUMMARY (debiased, pre-cutoff sources only):
Here are the sources that strictly predate that cutoff: • **[February 26, 2026]** Colombia's Petro Reaches Highest Approval Rating Since Taking Office — colombiaone.com — Gustavo Petro reached 49.1% approval according to Invamer survey, marking an 11.1-point jump from December 2025, with disapproval falling to 46.1%. --- **Note:** The other search results are dated March 14, March 26, and April 2026, which fall on or after the March 5, 2026 cutoff date you specified, so they have been excluded per your instructions.

args.reasoning:
Key disagreement: 5 of the agents (Opus variants + GPT-5) cluster tightly around 0.74-0.81 for Government Alliance, while DEEP_2 is an outlier at 0.45 (favoring Opposition), and DEEP_0/DEEP_1 are moderate at 0.62-0.67. This is an INTERPRETIVE disagreement about how much weight to give polling leads (4-7 pts for Historic Pact) vs. structural concerns about Colombian opposition fragmentation and historical incumbent under-performance.

My clarifying searches confirmed the pre-cutoff factual basis: Petro's approval surged to 49.1% in late Feb 2026 (highest of his term, +11.1 pts from Dec), and multiple polls (AtlasIntel, Guarumo) consistently show Historic Pact leading Democratic Center by 4-7 points with superior seat projections (20-25 vs 17-19). The DEEP_2 outlier appears to be relying on stale 2024 data and didn't update on the Feb 2026 approval surge — I resist being dragged toward it per AIA guidance.

Kalshi market is at 0.69, slightly below the agent cluster mean (~0.72). The polling + approval evidence supports the cluster view. I'll land near the cluster mean, slightly tempered by genuine uncertainty around Colombian polling reliability and the Senate's PR/closed-list dynamics that could allow fragmented opposition to still aggregate. Medium confidence — search confirmed but didn't dramatically shift view.
